items.mistake: Assuming Var(X + Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) without checking dependence
items.fix: Use Var(X + Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) + 2 Cov(X, Y); variances add only when Cov(X, Y) = 0 (e.g. independent or uncorrelated).
items.why: Ignoring covariance double-counts or under-counts combined variability—critical when summing correlated feature contributions or ensemble errors.
items.example: If X and Y are identical (Y = X), Var(X + Y) = Var(2X) = 4 Var(X), not 2 Var(X).

items.mistake: Applying linearity of expectation to variance or to E[XY]
items.fix: Keep E[aX + bY] = aE[X] + bE[Y] for expectations only; for products use E[XY] = Cov(X,Y) + E[X]E[Y], and see the covariance topic for Cov.
items.why: Treating variance or products as linear leads to wrong risk estimates and incorrect feature interaction math.
items.example: If X ~ Bernoulli(0.5), E[X²] = 0.5 but (E[X])² = 0.25; Var(X) = 0.5 − 0.25 = 0.25, not zero.

items.mistake: Using population variance formula with divisor n on sample data when unbiasedness matters
items.fix: Use sample variance s² = (1/(n−1)) Σ (xᵢ − x̄)² for unbiased estimation of Var(X); use divisor n only when defining MSE on a fixed batch.
items.why: Underestimating variance with divisor n shrinks confidence intervals and misstates feature spread in small datasets.
items.example: For n = 2 points [1, 5], divisor n gives variance 4; divisor n−1 gives 8—the latter is the unbiased estimate of population variance.

items.fix: Compute E[g(X)] as Σ g(x)p(x) or ∫ g(x)f(x)dx; equality g(E[X]) = E[g(X)] holds only for linear g (unless X is degenerate).
items.why: Jensen's inequality violations appear everywhere—e.g. E[1/X] ≠ 1/E[X]—breaking naive plug-in estimates in transforms and log-space metrics.
